narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school journey, including your undergraduate studies where you earned a 3.00 GPA, scored 35 on the LSAT, and ranked in the 6th decile for your first-year school grades and 9th decile for your third-year performance, while planning to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicted a 64% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval, leading to the regrettable rejection of your application. This prediction stems from a careful analysis of how your profile stacks up against thousands of past students' outcomes. The most influential factor in your case was actually your strong 9th-decile ranking in third-year school grades, which worked strongly in your favor by suggesting you'd likely pass the bar. However, your undergraduate GPA had the next biggest impact, pushing the prediction toward failure because it's a bit below the typical 3.21 average seen in successful applicants. Your first-year decile of 6th also contributed toward the failure prediction, as it's slightly above the average and signals middling early performance compared to peers who passed. Your LSAT score played a moderate role in the same direction, being under the 36.7 average that stronger candidates typically have. Less influential but still notable were your family income bracket, which nudged toward failure relative to dataset norms, and your full-time status, which had minimal sway since most students (over 92%) share it. Overall, while your later academic strengths shine, the model weighed your earlier metrics and test scores as bigger risks for bar success than what's common among admitted students who passed. I know this is disappointing, especially given your dedication, but it's not the end of the road—consider retaking the LSAT or bolstering your GPA through post-bac courses to shift these key factors. Reach out to advisors for targeted prep programs proven to boost bar passage rates, and reapply once you've strengthened those areas; many students in similar situations have turned predictions around. We're rooting for your success.
